hints.junior: Any env var starting with REACT_APP_, VITE_, or NEXT_PUBLIC_ is in your JavaScript bundle. Open DevTools Sources and search — you can find it in seconds.
hints.mid: Minification is not security. Any determined person (or bot scanner) can extract a key from a minified bundle in under a minute.
hints.staff: Gitleaks and GitHub secret scanning catch keys in git history. A key that was ever committed — even if removed — should be rotated. Git history is permanent.

hints.junior: The broken demo has a fake API key in the bundle. Search for 'KEY' in DevTools Sources — you'll find it. The fixed version makes the same call through /api/proxy.
hints.mid: Check the Network tab request URLs. Broken: key is a URL parameter (visible in logs, proxies, CDNs). Fixed: URL is /api/proxy, key is on the server.
hints.staff: Run strings on the production bundle to verify no key leaked. This is a good addition to your deployment CI checklist.

hints.junior: The concept chapter is the principle — read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
hints.mid: Connect the principle to your component API — what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
hints.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

hints.junior: Skim the code patterns here — you can copy the structure even if the syntax is new.
hints.mid: Compare broken vs fixed implementation — the diff is usually smaller than you expect.
hints.staff: Check edge cases: SSR, hydration, design tokens, and high-contrast mode.

anecdotes.junior: A freelance client got a $3,000 AWS bill from a key they put in a React app. The key was scraped within hours of deployment. Key rotation + proxy fixed it.
anecdotes.mid: The pattern I see most often: team uses NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix 'because it needs to work on the client.' The fix: add a Next.js API route as a proxy — 10 minutes of work.
anecdotes.staff: Secret scanning bots are automated. GitHub Actions, npm publish, and Vercel deployments are all monitored by scanners looking for leaked API keys. Assume anything public is scanned.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're fixing a security mistake that costs real money: putting API keys in frontend JavaScript where anyone can read them.
sections.mid: This case covers the secrets-in-bundle vulnerability — why client-side JavaScript is public, the proxy pattern, and serverless functions as a lightweight fix.
sections.staff: We're covering OWASP A02 cryptographic failures, the full secret management lifecycle (rotation, CI scanning, rate limiting), and framework-specific patterns for keeping secrets server-side.
